_meta.description: Coursera-style learning content for CyberAI. Each module has a left-panel list of learning items (readings + videos) grouped under a section, plus the existing quiz. Item types: 'reading' (rich text content rendered in the main panel) and 'video' (embedded YouTube). VIDEO LINKS BELOW ARE STARTING PLACEHOLDERS from reputable channels - VERIFY each loads and REPLACE with your own/preferred videos before submitting. estMinutes drives the '• X min' label. The first video per module is the primary explainer.
_meta.videoEmbedNote: Embed via https://www.youtube.com/embed/{videoId}. Store only the videoId; build the URL in code. Replace any that don't load.
_meta.completionNote: Each item can be marked complete ('Mark as completed'); a module's lessons are 'done' when all items are complete. This is separate from quiz pass (70%). Both lesson-complete and quiz-pass can be shown; certificate gating stays on quiz pass per SCORING.md.

body.text: Welcome to the Phishing module. Phishing is the most common cyber attack in the world, and it works by targeting people rather than computers. In this module you'll learn to recognise the lures attackers use, understand why they work, and build the habits that keep you and your organisation safe.
What is phishing?

Phishing is a fraudulent attempt to obtain sensitive information — usernames, passwords, card details — by disguising as a trustworthy entity in an electronic communication, most often email. The attacker's goal is to get you to click a malicious link, open an infected attachment, or hand over information directly.

Phishing succeeds because it exploits human psychology: trust, urgency, fear, and curiosity. A well-crafted phishing email doesn't look like an attack — it looks like a routine message from your bank, a colleague, or a service you use every day.
Two-thirds of data breaches involve a human element. Learning to spot phishing is one of the highest-impact security skills you can develop.

items.title: Phishing explained
items.videoSource: Security Quotient — 'Phishing: A game of deception'
items.description: A short animated explainer on how phishing attacks deceive users and what to watch for.

items.title: Red flags of a phishing email

body.text: Most phishing emails share a set of tell-tale signs. Learn these and you'll spot the majority of attacks before they do any harm.

items.title: Urgent or threatening language
items.text: 'Account suspended', 'Unauthorized login', 'Act within 24 hours'. Manufactured urgency rushes you into clicking before you think.

items.title: Generic greetings
items.text: 'Dear Customer' or 'Dear User' instead of your name can indicate a mass attack.

items.title: Mismatched sender address
items.text: The display name says 'PayPal' but the actual address is 'secure-update@paypal-service123.com'. Always check the full email address, not just the name.

items.title: Suspicious links
items.text: Hover (don't click) to reveal the true destination URL. Lookalike domains (paypa1.com) and shortened links hide malicious sites.

items.title: Unexpected attachments
items.text: Especially files with double extensions like 'invoice.pdf.exe'. Never open attachments you weren't expecting.

items.title: Spelling and grammar errors
items.text: Obvious mistakes can signal a scam — though polished phishing exists too, so absence of errors isn't proof of safety.

body.text: Attackers often combine several of these — urgency PLUS a suspicious link PLUS a request for information. The more red flags, the higher the risk.

Once you can spot a generic phishing email, attackers level up. These advanced variants are harder to detect because they are personalised, technically sophisticated, or exploit channels beyond your inbox.

items.title: Spear phishing
items.text: Personalised attacks using your name, role, company, or recent activity. Attackers gather this from LinkedIn, social media, and data breaches. The email feels like it was written specifically for you — because it was.

items.title: Whaling
items.text: Spear phishing aimed at executives (CEO, CFO, board members). The goal is large wire transfers, system access, or sensitive data. A convincing whaling email can cost an organisation millions.

items.title: Clone phishing
items.text: The attacker duplicates a legitimate email you previously received — same sender, same subject, same layout — and replaces the link or attachment with a malicious one. It appears to be a follow-up from a trusted source.

items.title: Quishing (QR code phishing)
items.text: Phishing via QR codes in emails or printed materials. QR codes hide the destination URL, bypassing most email link scanners. Never scan QR codes from unexpected emails.

items.title: Pharming
items.text: DNS poisoning redirects you to a fake website even when you type the correct URL. Your browser shows the right address but you're on the attacker's server.

items.title: Business Email Compromise (BEC)
items.text: Impersonation of an executive or supplier to redirect wire transfers or change payment details. Always verify payment changes through a separate, known phone number.

body.text: Spear phishing and BEC don't look like mass spam — they look like real emails from real people. Verification via a separate channel is the only reliable defence.
Behind the scenes, several technical standards and attack techniques determine whether a phishing email reaches you at all — and what attackers do after they've taken your credentials.

items.title: SPF, DKIM & DMARC
items.text: Email authentication standards. SPF lists authorised sending servers. DKIM adds a cryptographic signature to emails. DMARC instructs receivers to quarantine or reject messages that fail SPF/DKIM. A DMARC policy of 'reject' stops most spoofed emails reaching inboxes.

items.title: AiTM (Adversary-in-the-Middle) phishing
items.text: A reverse-proxy intercepts your login, passing your credentials to the real site to complete authentication — but capturing your session cookie too. This bypasses standard 2FA. Phishing-resistant hardware keys (FIDO2/passkeys) are the only defence.

items.title: Consent phishing / OAuth phishing
items.text: Instead of stealing your password, attackers trick you into granting a malicious app full access to your Google or Microsoft account. Review app permissions regularly and revoke any you don't recognise.

items.title: Tracking pixels
items.text: Tiny 1x1 images loaded from an attacker's server when you open an email, confirming your email is active and revealing your IP address and device. Disable automatic image loading in your email client for unknown senders.

body.text: AiTM attacks mean that even entering the correct password on what looks like a legitimate site can compromise your account. Always check browser security indicators and use hardware keys for critical accounts.
The golden rule: verify, don't trust

If an email seems suspicious, it probably is. Do not click links or download attachments. If you think a message might be legitimate, navigate to the company's website directly in a new browser tab — never via the email's link — and log in there.
If you clicked something
Don't panic, but act fast: disconnect from the internet to stop malware spreading, and report it to IT/Security immediately. Speed lets the security team contain any damage.
Always report

Reporting phishing — even attempts you didn't fall for — lets security analyse the email, block the sender, and protect everyone. Multiple reports help them gauge the scale of a campaign. Reporting is a team defense, not just a personal one.

Your password is your first and most crucial line of defense. Attackers use automated software that can attempt millions of guesses per second, so simple, common, or personal passwords can be cracked almost instantly.
The difference between a weak and a strong password is often the difference between a breached account and a safe one — and it costs you nothing to get right.

items.title: The pillars of a strong password
Length is strength
items.text: Aim for at least 14 characters. Each additional character makes a password exponentially harder to crack — length matters more than swapping in a single symbol.

items.title: Complexity helps
items.text: Mix uppercase, lowercase, numbers, and symbols. Avoid predictable patterns like 'word + year + !' (e.g. Summer2024!).

items.title: Uniqueness is non-negotiable
items.text: Never reuse passwords. A breach on one site exposes every account sharing that password — this is how 'credential stuffing' attacks work.

items.title: Passphrases work
items.text: A sequence of random words (correct-horse-battery-staple) is both long and memorable, making it strong yet practical.

items.title: Password managers explained
items.description: How password managers generate, store, and autofill unique strong passwords so you only remember one master password.

items.title: Your secret weapon: the password manager

body.text: It's impossible to remember dozens of unique, complex passwords — so don't try. A trusted password manager generates, stores, and autofills ultra-strong passwords for every account. You only memorise one master password.
Bonus protection: because a manager only autofills on the exact matching domain, it won't fill your credentials on a lookalike phishing site — giving you a silent warning that something is wrong.

items.title: Two-Factor Authentication explained
items.description: What 2FA is and why a second factor protects you even if your password is stolen.

body.text: Understanding how attackers crack passwords helps you understand why certain habits — unique passwords, length over complexity, a password manager — matter so much.

items.title: Brute force
items.text: Automated tools try every possible character combination. An 8-character password can be cracked in hours; a 16-character passphrase takes centuries. Length is your single best protection.

items.title: Dictionary attacks
items.text: Attackers use wordlists of common passwords, words, names, and known substitutions (p@ssw0rd, L3t_me_1n). Avoid any word you'd find in a dictionary, even with substitutions.

items.title: Credential stuffing
items.text: When a website is breached, attackers test those username/password pairs on every other major service. If you reuse passwords, one breach compromises everything. Unique passwords for every account — which a password manager makes easy — is the only fix.

items.title: Password spraying
items.text: Rather than hammering one account (which triggers lockout), attackers try one common password against thousands of accounts. This evades lockout policies. Avoid predictable patterns like 'Company2024' or 'Welcome1'.

items.title: Rainbow table attacks
items.text: Pre-computed tables of hash values allow instant lookup of unsalted passwords. Systems add a unique random 'salt' to each password before hashing specifically to defeat rainbow tables — which is why modern password storage is far more secure than older systems.

body.text: The most effective action you can take: use a password manager to generate and store a unique, long (20+ character) random password for every account.

items.title: Advanced authentication: passkeys, SSO & SIM swapping

body.text: Authentication is evolving. Understanding the latest attack techniques and the technology designed to defeat them keeps you ahead of attackers.

items.title: MFA fatigue / push bombing
items.text: An attacker who has your password sends repeated push notification approval requests to your phone, hoping you'll tap 'Approve' to make them stop. If you receive unexpected 2FA prompts, deny them and change your password immediately.

items.title: Passkeys
items.text: Passkeys replace passwords entirely using public-key cryptography. A private key stays on your device; a public key is on the website. You authenticate with your fingerprint or PIN locally — the private key never leaves your device, making passkeys completely phishing-resistant.

items.title: Single Sign-On (SSO)
items.text: One login (Google, Microsoft, Apple ID) grants access to many services. Enormously convenient, but it means your SSO account is extraordinarily valuable. Protect it with the strongest 2FA available — preferably a hardware security key.

items.title: SIM swapping
items.text: An attacker calls your mobile carrier, provides personal details gathered from social media or data breaches, and convinces them to port your number to a new SIM. They then receive all your SMS messages — including 2FA codes — and can reset your passwords. Use authenticator apps or hardware keys, never SMS 2FA, for important accounts.

items.title: Backup & recovery codes
items.text: When enabling 2FA, services provide single-use recovery codes. Print them and store them physically in a safe place. Never photograph them or store them in cloud notes — these codes bypass 2FA entirely and are as valuable as your password.

body.text: If you receive 2FA prompts you didn't initiate, treat it as an active attack. Deny the request, change your password, and check for suspicious account activity immediately.

2FA is a security force-multiplier. It requires a second form of verification — like a code from your phone or a hardware key — in addition to your password. Even if an attacker steals your password, they can't get in without the second factor.

items.title: Hardware keys are strongest
items.text: FIDO2/U2F security keys resist phishing and SIM-swap attacks.

items.title: Authenticator apps are great
items.text: TOTP apps generate codes on your device, not tied to your phone number — safer than SMS.

items.title: SMS is better than nothing
items.text: But it's vulnerable to SIM swapping. Use app or hardware 2FA where you can.

body.text: If you ever receive a 2FA code you didn't request, someone likely has your password. Don't share the code — change your password immediately.

Malware is any software designed to harm your system — it includes viruses, worms, trojans, spyware, and more. Ransomware is a particularly damaging type that encrypts all your files and holds them hostage until you pay a fee.

items.title: Virus
items.text: Attaches to files and spreads when those files are run.

items.title: Worm
items.text: Self-replicates across networks without needing a host file or user action.

items.title: Trojan
items.text: Disguised as legitimate software to trick you into running it.

items.title: Spyware
items.text: Secretly collects information like keystrokes and credentials.

items.title: Ransomware
items.text: Encrypts your files and demands payment for the decryption key.

items.title: Ransomware explained
items.description: A concise overview of how ransomware works and how to defend against it.

body.text: Knowing how malware gets in is half the battle. The vast majority of infections arrive through a small number of routes.

items.title: Phishing emails
items.text: Clicking malicious links or opening infected attachments is the #1 way malware spreads.

items.title: Malicious downloads
items.text: 'Free' software, games, or cracked/pirated apps from untrusted sites are frequently bundled with malware.

items.title: Unpatched software
items.text: Attackers exploit security holes in outdated operating systems, browsers, and apps. Keeping everything updated closes these doors.

items.title: Infected USB drives
items.text: 'Baiting' attacks leave malicious drives for curious people to plug in.

items.title: More malware types you should know

body.text: The malware landscape is far broader than viruses and ransomware. Understanding these additional types helps you recognise unusual system behaviour as a potential infection.

items.title: Rootkits
items.text: Hide deep in the operating system, often below the level antivirus can reach. They give attackers persistent, privileged access and can survive reboots. Detection often requires specialised bootable scanners or full OS reinstallation.

items.title: Keyloggers
items.text: Record every keystroke — capturing passwords, credit card numbers, private messages, and search queries. They can be software-installed via phishing or hardware-based (a physical device between keyboard and computer). Signs include unexpected network traffic.

items.title: Botnets
items.text: Your infected device joins a remotely controlled army of compromised machines used to send spam, launch DDoS attacks, or mine cryptocurrency. Your machine works for criminals while you bear the cost.

items.title: Cryptojacking
items.text: Malware silently uses your CPU or GPU to mine cryptocurrency for the attacker. Signs: unusually high CPU usage, slow performance, device running hot, and high electricity bills. Can arrive via malicious browser scripts (no installation needed) or installed malware.

items.title: Fileless malware
items.text: Lives entirely in memory — no file written to disk, making it invisible to traditional antivirus. It uses legitimate OS tools like PowerShell to carry out attacks. Behaviour-based Endpoint Detection and Response (EDR) is required to catch it.

items.title: Logic bombs
items.text: Dormant code hidden in legitimate software that activates when a specific condition is met — a date, a user action, or a file being deleted. Often planted by malicious insiders. Terminated employees' access should be revoked immediately.

body.text: Unusual system behaviour — high CPU with no obvious cause, unexpected network traffic, disabled security tools — should always be reported to IT even if you're not sure. Let the experts investigate.
Modern malware attacks are increasingly sophisticated, industrialised, and targeted. Understanding these techniques helps you appreciate why layered defences and fast reporting are so critical.

items.title: Zero-day vulnerabilities
items.text: A software flaw unknown to the vendor — no patch exists. Attackers exploit zero-days before any defence is possible. Keeping software updated eliminates known vulnerabilities; behaviour-based security catches exploitation attempts for unknown ones.

items.title: Ransomware-as-a-Service (RaaS)
items.text: Criminal groups sell ransomware toolkits like a franchise. Non-technical criminals pay a subscription, launch attacks, and split ransoms with the developers. This has dramatically lowered the barrier to entry and explains the surge in attacks.

items.title: Double extortion
items.text: Modern ransomware steals data before encrypting it. Even if you restore from backup, attackers threaten to publish sensitive data publicly. Backup alone is no longer sufficient — you must also prevent the initial breach.

items.title: Supply chain attacks
items.text: Malware injected into a legitimate software update or third-party library you trust. The SolarWinds attack compromised thousands of organisations via a trusted software update. Vet suppliers and monitor for unexpected behaviour after updates.

items.title: Lateral movement
items.text: After infecting one machine, attackers probe your network to reach more valuable systems — spreading like a disease. Network segmentation, least-privilege accounts, and monitoring unusual internal connections limit how far they spread.

items.title: Indicators of Compromise (IoC)
items.text: Signs of infection: unexplained CPU/network spikes, new unknown processes, disabled antivirus, unusual outbound connections, or files appearing/disappearing. Report any of these immediately — early detection dramatically limits damage.

body.text: If ransomware strikes: isolate the infected machine from the network immediately, do NOT pay the ransom, report to IT and law enforcement, and restore from clean offline backups.
A multi-layered defense is key. No single control stops everything, so combine several.

items.title: Back up regularly
items.text: Follow the 3-2-1 rule: 3 copies of your data, on 2 different media types, with 1 copy offsite/offline. Clean backups are your best defense against ransomware — they let you restore without paying.

items.title: Patch everything
items.text: Keep your OS, browser, and apps fully updated so known vulnerabilities can't be exploited.

items.title: Use reputable antivirus/EDR
items.text: Keep definitions current; modern EDR adds behaviour-based detection and response.

items.title: Work with least privilege
items.text: Use a standard (non-admin) account day-to-day so malware that runs is limited in what it can do.

items.title: Be cautious
items.text: Don't enable macros from unexpected documents; don't plug in unknown USB drives.

body.text: Should you pay the ransom? Generally no — it funds crime and doesn't guarantee recovery. Rely on backups and involve IT and, where appropriate, law enforcement.

vishing-smishing.sectionSubtitle: Phishing beyond the inbox: calls and texts

body.text: Social engineering doesn't only happen in your inbox. Attackers also use phone calls and text messages to trick you. These are known as Vishing (Voice Phishing) and Smishing (SMS Phishing).

items.title: Vishing
items.text: Voice phishing — scams conducted over phone calls.

items.title: Smishing
items.text: SMS phishing — fraudulent text messages.

body.text: Phishing, vishing, and smishing are all the same idea — social engineering — differing only in the channel: email, voice, or SMS.

items.title: Urgent bank alerts (smishing)
items.text: A text claims your account is frozen and links to a fake 'verify your identity' page that steals your login.

items.title: Tech support scams (vishing)
items.text: An unsolicited call from 'Microsoft' or 'Apple' claims your computer is infected, aiming to get remote access or payment.

items.title: Delivery-fee smishing
items.text: A text says a package delivery failed and asks for a small 're-delivery fee' via a link.

items.title: Caller ID spoofing
items.text: Attackers fake the displayed number — even your bank's — so caller ID is never proof of identity.

items.title: Emergency/impersonation
items.text: A caller pretends to be a relative in trouble, pressuring you for money and secrecy.

items.title: Vishing & smishing awareness
items.description: How scammers use texts and calls to harvest personal information, and how to respond.

body.text: Artificial intelligence has made vishing and smishing dramatically more convincing. These techniques were once the domain of sophisticated criminal groups — now they're widely available.

items.title: Voice cloning & deepfake audio
items.text: AI can clone a person's voice from just a few seconds of audio. Attackers clone executives, family members, or bank representatives and call you in real time. If a caller makes an urgent financial or access request, hang up and call the person back on a number you already have.

items.title: OTP bots
items.text: Automated systems call you pretending to be your bank, asking you to 'confirm' the one-time passcode just sent to your phone — relaying that code to an attacker logging in simultaneously. No legitimate organisation will ever ask you to read back a code you just received.

items.title: Hybrid smishing-to-vishing
items.text: A text message creates alarm ('Your account is at risk — call this number immediately'), then the follow-up call does the social engineering. The two-step process feels legitimate because you 'initiated' the call.

items.title: Pig butchering scams
items.text: Attackers build a relationship over weeks or months via text or messaging apps — posing as a romantic interest or new friend — before introducing an 'exclusive' crypto investment. Once money is invested, they vanish. The extended trust-building phase is what makes this devastatingly effective.

body.text: If anyone — including someone who sounds exactly like your manager or a family member — makes an urgent request for money or access credentials via a call you didn't initiate, hang up and verify via a known number.
Phone-based scams are constantly evolving. Familiarity with these patterns helps you recognise them before you become a victim.

items.title: Wangiri fraud
items.text: A missed call from an unfamiliar international number. Calling back connects to a premium-rate line, racking up large per-minute charges. Never return missed calls from numbers you don't recognise, especially from abroad.

items.title: Reverse vishing
items.text: Attackers send a fake alert — via email or SMS — with a 'security hotline' number to call. You call them, believing it's safe because you initiated it. Always find contact numbers from official websites, not from messages.

items.title: QR code smishing
items.text: SMS messages containing a QR code image instead of a text link. The URL is hidden inside the image, bypassing most SMS filters. Treat QR codes in unsolicited messages with the same scepticism as unknown links.

items.title: SIM swapping
items.text: An attacker calls your mobile carrier, provides personal details (gathered from social media or breaches), and convinces them to port your number to a new SIM. They then receive all your SMS messages — including 2FA codes — and can reset passwords. Protect against this by using app-based or hardware 2FA instead of SMS.

body.text: The common thread across all phone scams: urgency + a request for action (call back, click, share a code, transfer money). Pause, verify independently, and never act on information provided in the suspicious message itself.
Never trust, always verify

Do not click links in suspicious texts. Hang up on unsolicited calls. If a message claims to be from a company you use, contact them directly using a phone number or website you know is legitimate — never the contact details provided in the message.

items.title: Don't share OTPs
items.text: No legitimate organisation will ask you to read out a one-time passcode.

items.title: Don't press buttons on robocalls
items.text: Responding confirms your number is active and invites more scams.

items.title: Call back on a known number
items.text: Use the number on your card or the official website to verify any real issue.

items.title: Beware gift-card requests
items.text: No real agency or boss legitimately demands payment in gift cards.

physical-security.sectionSubtitle: Protecting devices and data in the real world

body.text: Protecting information goes beyond firewalls and passwords. Physical security means protecting your devices and your screen from unauthorised access in the real world — at the office, while travelling, and when working remotely.
A locked laptop on an encrypted drive is far safer than a powerful firewall protecting an unlocked, unattended machine.

items.title: In the office and on the go
Lock your screen
items.text: Always lock when you step away, even for a moment. Windows: Win+L. Mac: Cmd+Ctrl+Q. Make it a reflex.

items.title: Beware shoulder surfing
items.text: In cafes, airports, and trains, people can watch you type passwords or read sensitive data. A privacy screen filter narrows the viewing angle.

items.title: Watch for tailgating
items.text: Don't let unknown people follow you through secure doors. Direct them to reception/security instead.

items.title: Secure your devices
items.text: Never leave laptops or phones unattended in public. When travelling, keep them with you, not in checked luggage.

items.title: Clean desk policy
items.text: Clear sensitive papers and media when you leave your desk.

items.title: Public Wi-Fi & VPN safety
items.description: Why public Wi-Fi is risky and how a VPN protects your connection.

body.text: Physical security controls protect the people, equipment, and data within your organisation. Understanding these measures helps you work within them effectively and spot when they're being bypassed.

items.title: Full-disk encryption
items.text: If a laptop is stolen, full-disk encryption (BitLocker on Windows, FileVault on Mac) makes data completely unreadable without the correct login credentials. Without it, data can be read by booting from a USB drive. Ensure it's enabled on all work devices.

items.title: RFID badge cloning
items.text: Access card readers that use proximity (RFID) can be skimmed from up to 30cm away using a hidden device in a pocket or bag. Anti-passback systems help by preventing a card from being used to enter if it wasn't used to exit — flagging cloned badges.

items.title: Mantrap / vestibule
items.text: A two-door airlock entry where only one door opens at a time. Used in high-security areas — you must be positively identified before the inner door opens, preventing tailgating completely.

items.title: Visitor management
items.text: All visitors should sign in with valid ID, receive a temporary visitor badge, and be escorted at all times in restricted areas. Never badge a visitor through without following the process — regardless of how official they appear.

items.title: Dumpster diving
items.text: Attackers search physical bins for printed documents, sticky notes, org charts, and customer data. Always shred sensitive documents before disposal using a cross-cut shredder. Never put confidential materials in recycling or general waste.

items.title: Printer output tray risk
items.text: Sensitive documents are frequently forgotten in shared printer output trays, visible to anyone who walks past. Collect prints immediately. Where available, use 'follow-you' printing — you authenticate at the printer before the job releases.

body.text: Physical security is as important as digital security. An attacker who can walk into your building has already bypassed most of your technical controls.
Discarding or repurposing devices without proper data removal is one of the most common and avoidable sources of data breaches.

items.title: Secure media destruction
items.text: Deleting files or formatting a drive does not remove data — it only removes directory pointers. Secure disposal requires: software overwriting (writing random data multiple passes), degaussing (powerful magnets for magnetic drives), or physical destruction (shredding). Your IT team should handle this for all work devices.

items.title: IT asset disposal policy
items.text: Every device — including old PCs, mobiles, USB drives, and printers — must be formally decommissioned and wiped before leaving the organisation. A device sold or donated without proper wiping can expose years of sensitive business and personal data.

items.title: OSINT from discarded materials
items.text: Open Source Intelligence techniques can reconstruct org charts, internal projects, technology stacks, and customer names from discarded printouts, whiteboard photos, and even post-it notes. Physical information hygiene matters as much as digital hygiene.

body.text: Never dispose of work devices yourself. Always return them to IT for formal decommissioning — this protects both you and the organisation.
Public Wi-Fi is not secure. Attackers on the same network can potentially intercept your data, and 'evil twin' hotspots mimic legitimate networks to lure you in.

items.title: Avoid sensitive logins
items.text: Don't access your bank or other sensitive accounts on public Wi-Fi unless protected.

items.title: Use a VPN
items.text: A VPN encrypts ALL your traffic, creating a secure tunnel that stops snoopers on the same network.

items.title: Treat every unknown network as untrusted
items.text: A 'zero trust' mindset drives safer choices by default.

items.title: Avoid juice jacking
items.text: Don't charge at unknown public USB ports — use a wall adapter or a charge-only cable.

data-handling.sectionSubtitle: Classifying, protecting, and disposing of data

body.text: Data is classified by its sensitivity so that protection matches the risk. You have a responsibility to protect sensitive data appropriately.

items.title: Public
items.text: Press releases, marketing material — safe to share openly.

items.title: Internal
items.text: Org charts, internal memos — for staff, not the public.

items.title: Confidential / PII
items.text: Personally Identifiable Information (names, addresses, IDs), financial and health records — must be strongly protected to prevent identity theft and privacy violations.

items.title: Need to know
items.text: Only access and share sensitive data with those who have a legitimate business need.

items.title: Least privilege
items.text: Users should have only the access required for their role — reducing damage if an account is compromised.

items.title: Secure storage
items.text: Keep confidential data on approved, encrypted systems — not personal devices or unsanctioned cloud accounts.

items.title: Encrypt in transit and at rest
items.text: Use secure, encrypted methods to store and send sensitive data.

items.title: Proper disposal
items.text: Shred physical documents (cross-cut). Securely wipe or destroy digital media — deleting to the trash isn't enough.

items.title: Use BCC for group external email
items.text: Putting external recipients in CC exposes everyone's address — a PII leak.

items.title: Data protection & GDPR basics
items.description: A plain-English overview of data protection principles and why compliance matters.

body.text: Data protection law directly affects how you handle personal information at work. Understanding these concepts keeps your organisation compliant and protects the people whose data you process.
The CIA Triad

The foundation of all information security: Confidentiality (only authorised people can access data), Integrity (data is accurate and unmodified), and Availability (data is accessible when needed). Every security control maps to one or more of these.

items.title: Right to erasure (right to be forgotten)
items.text: Under GDPR, individuals can request deletion of their personal data. Organisations must comply within one month unless a legal basis for retention exists. This obligation extends to backups and archives.

items.title: Data Subject Access Request (DSAR)
items.text: Any individual can request a copy of all personal data held about them. Organisations must respond within one month, free of charge for the first request. Failing to respond is a GDPR violation.

items.title: Data Protection Officer (DPO)
items.text: Organisations processing large volumes or sensitive categories of personal data must appoint a DPO — responsible for ensuring compliance and advising on data protection matters. Know who your DPO is.

items.title: Data Protection Impact Assessment (DPIA)
items.text: Required before starting high-risk processing activities — large-scale profiling, processing special category data, or systematic monitoring. A DPIA identifies and mitigates privacy risks proactively.

items.title: Controller vs Processor
items.text: The data controller decides why and how personal data is processed. The data processor handles it on the controller's behalf. Both have GDPR obligations; their relationship must be governed by a written Data Processing Agreement.

items.title: Purpose limitation
items.text: Data collected for one purpose cannot be used for a different purpose without fresh consent or another legal basis. Email addresses collected for order confirmations cannot be used for marketing without separate consent.

items.title: Privacy by design
items.text: Privacy protections should be built into systems from the start, not added later. Default settings should offer maximum privacy — users should opt in to less private options, not be forced to opt out.

body.text: GDPR fines can reach €20 million or 4% of global annual turnover — whichever is higher. Compliance is not optional.

Even with strong policies, data can leave an organisation through technical failures, misconfigured tools, or well-intentioned workarounds. These topics cover practical controls and the risks of going outside approved systems.

items.title: Data Loss Prevention (DLP)
items.text: Software that monitors and controls data movement — blocking sensitive files from being emailed externally, uploaded to personal cloud storage, or copied to USB drives. DLP is a safety net that catches accidental data leaks and policy violations.

items.title: Shadow IT
items.text: Any application, service, or device used for work without IT approval — personal Dropbox, consumer WhatsApp for work messages, or a free SaaS tool. Shadow IT creates uncontrolled copies of organisational data outside security controls, often in breach of GDPR.

items.title: Data sovereignty
items.text: Data is subject to the laws of the country where it is physically stored. EU citizen personal data stored on US servers may violate GDPR unless specific safeguards (Standard Contractual Clauses, adequacy decisions) are in place.

items.title: Records of Processing Activities (ROPA)
items.text: A mandatory GDPR requirement for most organisations — a detailed record of all personal data processing: what's held, why, how long it's retained, who has access, and where it's stored. Regulators can request this at any time.

items.title: Pseudonymisation vs anonymisation
items.text: Pseudonymised data has identifiers replaced with codes — it's still personal data because re-identification is possible with the key. Truly anonymised data has identifiers irreversibly removed and falls outside GDPR. Most 'anonymised' datasets are actually pseudonymised.

body.text: Before using any new tool or service for work — especially cloud services — check with IT. What feels like a productivity boost may create a serious compliance and security liability.

items.title: When things go wrong: incident reporting

body.text: If you suspect a security incident — you clicked a phishing link, lost a work device, or see suspicious activity — report it immediately to the designated contact (IT, InfoSec, or your manager).

Fast reporting is critical to minimising damage. Many privacy laws (like GDPR) also require organisations to notify regulators and affected people within strict timeframes, so your prompt internal report enables a compliant response.
Never hide an incident to avoid blame. Time is the most valuable resource during a breach — early reporting protects everyone.

Social engineering is the art of manipulating people into giving up confidential information. Attackers know that humans are often the weakest link in the security chain — it's frequently easier to trick a person than to break technology.
Firewalls don't stop a convincing phone call. The best defense against social engineering is a skeptical, verify-first mindset.

items.title: What is social engineering?
items.description: How attackers manipulate trust, urgency, and fear to deceive their targets.

items.title: CEO fraud / BEC
items.text: An attacker impersonates an executive and urgently requests a wire transfer or sensitive data, usually targeting finance staff.

items.title: Deepfakes & AI voice cloning
items.text: Scammers use AI to clone a voice and call demanding immediate money or authorisation. Verify independently.

items.title: Baiting
items.text: Leaving an infected USB drive for a curious person to plug in.

items.title: Quid pro quo
items.text: Offering a benefit (fake 'IT help') in exchange for access or information.

items.title: Pretexting
items.text: Inventing a believable scenario — posing as a researcher, bank official, or new employee — to extract information.

items.title: Psychological levers
items.text: Authority, urgency, scarcity, social proof, and reciprocity are all used to lower your guard.

body.text: Before an attacker contacts you, they spend significant time learning about you and your organisation. This research phase — using publicly available information — is called Open Source Intelligence, or OSINT.

items.title: What attackers harvest
items.text: LinkedIn profiles reveal job titles, responsibilities, reporting relationships, tools used, projects underway, and colleagues' names. Company websites list executives and offices. Job postings reveal internal tech stacks. Press releases announce upcoming changes. GitHub repos can expose internal architecture.

items.title: Org chart harvesting
items.text: LinkedIn makes it easy to map who reports to whom, who approves financial transactions, and who has system access. Attackers use this to craft BEC emails — knowing exactly who to impersonate and who to target.

items.title: The LinkedIn oversharing problem
items.text: Posting about internal system migrations, new software deployments, project names, or upcoming restructures gives attackers perfect pretexts. 'We're deploying Salesforce next month' becomes a convincing cover for a fake Salesforce support call.

items.title: Simulated phishing tests
items.text: Most organisations run simulated phishing campaigns to identify training gaps. If you click a simulated link, you'll receive targeted training — not punishment. These tests show where defences are weakest before real attackers exploit them.

body.text: Before posting on LinkedIn or social media about your work, ask: could an attacker use this to craft a more convincing attack? Sharing project names, system migrations, or org changes may help attackers more than your network.

items.title: The six psychological principles attackers exploit

body.text: Social engineering works because it exploits fundamental aspects of human psychology. Psychologist Robert Cialdini identified six principles of influence — all of which attackers weaponise regularly.

items.title: Authority
items.text: People comply with requests from those in perceived authority. Attackers impersonate executives, IT teams, auditors, HMRC, or law enforcement. The antidote: verify identity through a separate, trusted channel regardless of how authoritative the contact seems.

items.title: Urgency
items.text: 'Act now or your account will be closed' / 'I need this before end of day.' Manufactured time pressure prevents critical thinking and verification. Legitimate requests can always wait for proper verification.

items.title: Scarcity
items.text: 'Only you can resolve this before the deadline.' Creating unique responsibility stops you from delegating, escalating, or double-checking.

items.title: Social proof
items.text: 'Everyone else in the department has already submitted theirs.' If others have complied, it seems safe and normal. Attackers fabricate consensus to lower your guard.

items.title: Liking & reciprocity
items.text: Attackers build rapport — complimenting your work, finding common ground — or perform a small favour before making their real request. Humans feel obligated to return favours, even to near-strangers.

items.title: Security fatigue
items.text: Bombarded with alerts, 2FA prompts, and access requests, people eventually approve things without reading them. Attackers send repeated push MFA notifications hoping exhaustion causes you to approve. Never approve authentication requests you didn't initiate.

body.text: When you notice any of these six principles being applied — especially in combination — treat it as a red flag. Legitimate contacts don't need to pressure you. Slow down, verify, and report if something feels off.

Always verify unusual requests — especially those involving money or sensitive data — through a secondary, trusted channel. If the 'CEO' emails for an urgent wire transfer, call them or speak in person to confirm.

items.title: Verify out-of-band
items.text: Confirm via a different, known channel — this defeats spoofing and impersonation.

items.title: Don't bypass procedures
items.text: Pressure to 'skip the rules just this once' is a major red flag.

items.title: Be helpful but cautious
items.text: Balance helpfulness with verification of sensitive or unusual requests.

items.title: Never plug in unknown devices
items.text: And be skeptical of unsolicited offers and 'support' that contacts you first.

items.title: Report attempts
items.text: Reporting helps security warn others and respond to active campaigns.

crypto-scams.sectionSubtitle: Protecting your money and digital assets

body.text: With the popularity of cryptocurrency, scammers have developed sophisticated methods to steal digital assets. Because crypto transactions are often irreversible and can be pseudonymous, they are a prime target for fraudsters — once funds are sent, they're usually gone.
Unlike a bank transfer, there is typically no 'undo' and no fraud department to claw back stolen crypto. Prevention is everything.

items.title: Pig butchering (investment scams)
items.text: Scammers build a long-term relationship (often romantic or friendly) online, then push you to invest in a fake crypto platform. They show fabricated profits to encourage bigger deposits before vanishing.

items.title: Fake exchanges & wallets
items.text: Malicious apps or websites mimic legitimate services. Once you deposit funds or enter your seed phrase, your assets are stolen.

items.title: Tech support & refund scams
items.text: You're told you're owed a refund or your account is compromised, then guided to transfer funds or grant remote access.

items.title: Giveaway scams
items.text: Fake 'celebrity' posts promise to double any crypto you send to an address. Crypto sent is never returned.

items.title: Rug pulls
items.text: Developers hype a token, attract investment, then drain liquidity and disappear.

items.title: Crypto scams explained
items.description: The most common crypto scams and how to avoid losing your funds.

body.text: Decentralised Finance (DeFi) offers powerful financial tools — but without the safeguards of traditional finance. Understanding these risks is essential before interacting with any DeFi protocol.

items.title: What DeFi means for risk
items.text: DeFi protocols run on smart contracts — self-executing code — with no central authority, no fraud department, and no customer support. If funds are stolen via an exploit or scam, there is typically no recourse. The code is the contract.

items.title: Smart contract vulnerabilities
items.text: Unaudited smart contracts often contain exploitable bugs. Attackers can drain entire liquidity pools in seconds. Only interact with protocols audited by reputable security firms — and even then, risk remains. Check audit reports on the protocol's official documentation.

items.title: Flash loan attacks
items.text: An attacker borrows a massive amount of cryptocurrency with no collateral (repaid within the same transaction), uses it to manipulate prices or exploit a protocol flaw, profits, and repays — all in milliseconds. A single transaction can drain millions from a protocol.

items.title: Yield farming scams
items.text: Promises of 100%, 500%, or 1000% APY are almost always Ponzi structures — 'yield' comes from new investors' money. When inflows stop, the protocol collapses. Early investors extract funds; everyone else loses everything.

items.title: Airdrop scams
items.text: You receive unexpected tokens in your wallet. To 'claim' them, a website asks you to connect your wallet and approve a transaction. That approval grants permission to drain your wallet. Never interact with tokens you didn't request or expect.

items.title: NFT scams & wash trading
items.text: Fake scarcity is manufactured by sellers trading NFTs between their own wallets to inflate price history, then selling to real buyers at inflated prices. Research unique buyers, transaction volume, and creator history before purchasing.

body.text: In DeFi, every wallet connection and transaction approval is a major security decision. If you don't understand exactly what a contract does, don't sign it.

Cryptocurrency transactions are irreversible. This means attackers have developed highly targeted techniques to manipulate individual transactions and wallet interactions. Understanding these protects your funds.

items.title: Address poisoning
items.text: An attacker sends a tiny transaction from a wallet address that looks almost identical to one you frequently use — differing only in a few middle characters. When you copy-paste from your transaction history, you might copy the attacker's address. Always verify the complete address before sending any transaction.

items.title: Clipboard malware
items.text: Malware monitors your clipboard and silently replaces any copied cryptocurrency address with the attacker's address. You paste what appears correct, verify only the first and last characters, and send funds to the attacker. Verify the full address character by character, or use QR codes from trusted sources only.

items.title: Wallet drainers
items.text: Malicious smart contracts that, once you approve a single transaction, gain unlimited permission to withdraw assets from your wallet. Commonly found on fake NFT minting sites, phishing pages, and fraudulent DeFi protocols. Treat every approval as permanent — because it is, unless revoked.

items.title: Token approval revocation
items.text: Every DeFi interaction may grant a contract permission to spend your tokens indefinitely. These approvals persist even if you stop using the protocol. Regularly audit and revoke unnecessary approvals using tools like Revoke.cash — a compromised protocol can drain your wallet through an old approval.

items.title: Dusting attacks
items.text: Attackers send tiny amounts of cryptocurrency (dust) to many wallets to trace transaction patterns and link addresses to real identities. Once they know a wallet belongs to a specific person, they target them with personalised scams or phishing.

items.title: QR code crypto scams
items.text: Fake 'wallet verification' or 'sync' websites instruct you to scan a QR code with your wallet app. The QR code initiates a transaction sending your funds to the attacker, or authorises a drainer contract. Never scan a QR code to 'verify', 'sync', or 'recover' a cryptocurrency wallet.

body.text: Hardware wallets protect your private keys — but they cannot protect you from approving a malicious transaction. Read every transaction prompt carefully before signing.
The one rule above all

Never share your wallet's seed phrase or private keys with anyone, ever. They are the master key to your funds. No legitimate exchange or support agent will ever ask for them — anyone who does is trying to steal from you.

items.title: Be skeptical of guarantees
items.text: 'Guaranteed' high returns or pressure to invest quickly are hallmarks of scams.

items.title: Use established exchanges
items.text: Stick to well-known, reputable platforms and verify URLs carefully before logging in.

items.title: Cold storage for large holdings
items.text: Keep significant crypto in a hardware wallet you control, with the seed phrase secured offline.

items.title: Enable 2FA
items.text: Protect exchange accounts so a stolen password alone isn't enough.

items.title: Double-check addresses
items.text: Clipboard-hijacking malware can swap addresses, and transactions can't be reversed.

body.text: If an offer sounds too good to be true — like doubling your money — it is a scam. Beware 'recovery services' too; they often re-target victims for another fee.
